draft.introduction: Matthew 20:1–16 presents the parable of the workers in the vineyard, where those hired last receive the same wage as those who labored all day. It overturns conventional notions of merit. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: For the kingdom of heaven is like a master of a house who went out early in the morning to hire workers for his vineyard. After agreeing with the workers for a denarius a day, he sent them into his vineyard.
translation.original_text: ομοια γαρ εστιν η βασιλεια των ουρανων ανθρωπω οικοδεσποτη

translation.text: But he replied to one of them, “Friend, I am doing you no wrong. Did you not agree with me for a denarius? I choose to give to this last worker as I give to you. Or do you begrudge my generosity?” So the last will be first, and the first last.

translator_notes.note: The Greek ophthalmos poneros, literally “evil eye,” is rendered here as “begrudge my generosity.” The parable asserts that divine grace is not distributed according to human accounting.
